fileLayout: models.py — domain entities, enums, value objects, custom exceptions (the "nouns")
strategies.py — Strategy interfaces + concretes (ONLY if §5 has a Strategy pattern)
gateways.py — external-system interfaces: PaymentGateway, NotificationSink, Clock (ONLY if §5 names one)

<domain>_service.py — the orchestrator: clean single-threaded core + ThreadSafe subclass (named after the DOMAIN, e.g. locker_service.py / parking_service.py / booking_service.py — NEVER service.py/utils.py/helpers.py)
main.py — small __main__ demo driver: 3-5 cases with print(), NO test framework
tests/test_<domain>.py — pytest suite: every public method, every error path, the concurrency case
tests/conftest.py — adds the parent dir to sys.path so `from models import …` works in tests
Total ~3-6 files. Workspace root: /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>/ where slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
recipe.jsonToolBlockContract:
EXACT field names — the frontend keys off these verbatim.

FILE BLOCKS (×5-6): "name": "write_file", "input": {"path": "<bare path>", "content": "..."}. The `path` is RELATIVE to the workspace — e.g. "models.py", "tests/test_booking.py" — NOT "workspace/<slug>/models.py", and the name is NOT "Write" and the key is NOT "file_path". Every file the candidate should see MUST be a write_file block (the LLD "Complete Code Implementation" tabbed viewer is built from them).

RUN BLOCKS (×2): "name": "run_command", "input": {"command": "python3 main.py"} — bare command, no `cd`. The two commands are "python3 main.py" and "python3 -m pytest -q".

Each tool block inside transcript[].blocks has shape: {"k": "tool", "id": "<id>", "name": "write_file"|"run_command", "input": {...}, "output": "<actual stdout / 'File created successfully.'>", "is_error": false}. Example ids: tool_write_0, tool_write_1, tool_write_2, tool_write_3, tool_write_4 (write_file blocks), tool_run_main and tool_run_pytest (run_command blocks). main.py stdout goes in the tool_run_main / tool_run block; pytest -q stdout goes in the tool_run_pytest block; both must be green.

TOP-LEVEL JSON: {"session_id": "<uuid>:lld", "mode": "lld", "title": "Design a <Problem Name>", "created_at": <unix ts>, "updated_at": <unix ts + 180>, "messages": [...], "transcript": [...]}. messages is Anthropic format: a user message {"role":"user","content":[{"type":"text","text":"<problem>"}]} and an assistant message {"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"<FULL §1-§9 markdown>"}]}. transcript[0] is {"role":"user","text":"Design a <Problem Name>","images":0}; transcript[1] is {"role":"assistant","thinking":"","blocks":[ {first block k:"text", md:"<FULL §1-§9 markdown>"}, then the write_file + run_command tool blocks ], "usage": {"input_tokens":9000,"output_tokens":16000,"cache_read_input_tokens":40000,"cache_creation_input_tokens":0}}. Text blocks use keys "k":"text" and "md".

FILENAME vs FIELD: JSON file is /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/<slug>.json where slug uses _lld (colon→underscore); the session_id field INSIDE the JSON keeps the colon form <uuid>:lld. Write via Python: out.write_text(json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2), encoding='utf-8').
sectionStructure: ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan (at the very top) — table (phase | what you do | mode) + the Mode A (whiteboard, no run) vs Mode B (coding env, run) note

§1 Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions — 1.1 plain-words restatement (coffee-chat tone); 1.2 candidate-LED dialogue (**You:** proposes assumption/scope-cut + asks to confirm, **Interviewer:** ~70% agrees, italic reaction line); 1.3 Final Requirements (numbered functional + "Out of scope" list) + 💬 opener
§2 Actors & Core Flows — bulleted actors + 3-5 one-line happy-path flows. No diagram here

§3 Core Entities (accept/reject) — 3.1 noun walkthrough accepting/rejecting each candidate noun with a reason; 3.2 entity table (Entity | Responsibility | Key fields typed | Invariant) + enums block; 3.3 🎙️ spoken-intro paragraph that names the rejected nouns

§4 Class Design — 4.1 per-class derivation (role → state-derivation table → operations-derivation table → design-choices Q&A); 4.2 consolidated classDiagram (draw-vs-say split) + relationships + Pattern Map + 🎙️ box-by-box + ⚠️ PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG
§5 Design Patterns & Principles — 5.1 Information Expert first; 5.2 applied patterns (1-3, each earned, with 💬 layman gloss); 5.3 SOLID tied to named classes; 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule

§5.5 Core Algorithms — A key insight (insight + why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it); B per-operation pseudocode + STORY-driven 🎙️ scripts + a DRY RUN of the pipeline method with a state-change table; C data-structure choices (4 cols incl. rejected alternative); D tricky logic (🧠 Claim / 🔬 Proof / 🛠️ Fix); E ONE "❌ what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example; F narration-order table

§6 Implementation — §6.0 Live Writing Order (9 phases + 7 golden rules + TL;DR time table + mid-flight situations), then orchestrator SKELETON first, then each method in the 6-block format (role / core-logic bullets / edge-case bullets / code / reasoning PROSE / 🎙️+⚠️), ONE Approach Comparison for the trickiest helper, helpers written (not skipped), and a Verification subsection with full-lifecycle state-table dry runs
§7 Putting It Together — 7.1 main.py narrated demo output; 7.2 tests/ pytest suite + the actual `pytest -q` output. Both green
§8 Key Flow — a sequenceDiagram of the most interesting flow + 🎙️ arrow-by-arrow narration + why-it-shows-the-design-is-good

§9 Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility — 4-step verbal race walkthrough, CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY table, CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A (why RLock, why not global, deadlock, pessimistic vs optimistic), the clean→thread-safe diff (Part A hook+subclass, Part B exact modified method, Part C barrier test proof), edge-cases-handled list, Extensibility (level-calibration note + summary table + 2-3 TWIST walkthroughs with minimal code diffs), and the hardest follow-up Q&A (concurrency/distributed/scale — distinct from the twists)
qualityRequirements: Canonical file layout enforced: models.py, <domain>_service.py, main.py, tests/test_<domain>.py, tests/conftest.py (+ strategies.py/gateways.py only if §5 needs them) — NO service.py/utils.py/helpers.py; service file named after the DOMAIN
Python 3.11+ idioms: list[Foo], X | None (not Optional[X]); NO `from __future__ import annotations`; imports at TOP only (no mid-file `import threading`)

models.py: all enums (IntEnum when ordering matters, else Enum); a SINGLE base exception <Domain>Error with one specific subclass per failure mode — never mix in a bare RuntimeError/Exception/ValueError; frozen dataclasses (@dataclass(frozen=True)) for value objects; mutable dataclasses for entities (state-machine entity owns its own transition methods)

<domain>_service.py: clean single-threaded service with a @contextmanager _lock() no-op hook; a ThreadSafe<Domain>Service(<Domain>Service) subclass overriding _lock() with the per-key RLock double-lock idiom; every public method from §6 fully implemented (NO pass/... bodies); no dead code (every method called from main.py or a test)
main.py: `# Python 3.11+` as the first line; narrated demo of 3-5 cases with print("Case N … -> OK") and plain `assert X, message` (no try/except False antipattern)

Concurrency test with STRICT assertions: len(wins) == K, len(set(wins)) == K (unique reservation ids), len({w.compartment_id …}) == K (UNIQUE resources — catches the silent collision bug), every loss is the typed error, and count of in-RESERVED/OCCUPIED resources == K; use threading.Barrier so all N threads hit the critical section at one instant; pytest.raises(<TypedError>) — never bare Exception
python3 main.py exits 0 with all cases printing OK; python3 -m pytest -q fully green; if either errors, fix the CODE (not the test) and re-run before continuing
NO `...` ANYWHERE in §6 code; no abbreviations, no `# ... rest of implementation`; every method body complete (every line, real argument, real error message, real return value)
Code blocks tagged ```python copy so the frontend renders a copy button

The §6 narration shows code method-by-method (teaching); the FULL files ALSO ship as write_file tool blocks — the frontend's LLD 'Complete Code Implementation' tabbed viewer renders from those write_file captures

Output must match every section (§1 through §9) of the production LLD system prompt at /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/lld_prompt.md, including the ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan at the top; the full §1–§9 markdown must be in the first text block (complete, not abbreviated)
§6 includes §6.0 Live Writing Order, orchestrator skeleton-first, the 6-block per-method format, ONE Approach Comparison, and a Verification dry-run subsection
§9 includes the Concurrency Vocabulary table, the Concurrency Follow-up Q&A, and 2-3 Extensibility TWIST walkthroughs
recipeSteps: Step 0 (prereq) — Read the full LLD system prompt at /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/lld_prompt.md before generating anything; the output must match its §1–§9 structure
Step 1 — Pick a session ID: a fresh UUID4 in the format <uuid>:lld. Compute slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id) (so :lld → _lld)

Step 2 — Set up workspace structure under /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>/ using the EXACT canonical file names (~3-6 files), with the models.py / <domain>_service.py / main.py / tests content rules
Step 3 — Generate the full §1–§9 LLD response as markdown (the complete, non-abbreviated text the user reads), matching the current structure: ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan + §1..§9 exactly

Step 4 — Run BOTH `cd /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/workspace/<slug>` then `python3 main.py` and `python3 -m pytest -q`; capture exact stdout from both (main → tool_run Bash block, pytest → tool_pytest Bash block); both must be green or fix the CODE and re-run

Step 5 — Write the conversation JSON to /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/<slug>.json with the documented structure: session_id (colon form), mode 'lld', title, created_at/updated_at, messages (Anthropic format), and transcript (text block first, then write_file ×5-6 + run_command ×2). Write via Python json.dumps(..., ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

Step 6 — Verify the JSON loads: `from backend.config import session_slug, CONV_DIR`, assert the file exists at CONV_DIR/<slug>.json, assert d['session_id'] == sid and d['mode'] == 'lld', then print the open URL https://localhost:8002/?s=<uuid>:lld

Final — Run the Quality checklist before finishing (file layout, 3.11 idioms, green main/pytest, unique-resource concurrency assert, single exception base, no ... in §6, §6.0/§9 required subsections, JSON exists, session_id colon form, slug _lld filename, all blocks use write_file/run_command with bare relative paths, full markdown in first text block, config loadability check)
gotchas: CRITICAL tool-block naming: frontend keys off EXACT names — use write_file / run_command, never Write / Bash. Use `path` (bare/relative) not `file_path`, and `command` not other keys. Bare relative paths like models.py and tests/test_booking.py — NEVER workspace/<slug>/models.py.

Slug vs session_id mismatch trap: the JSON FILENAME uses _lld (colon replaced by underscore) but the session_id field INSIDE the JSON keeps the colon (<uuid>:lld). The open URL also uses the colon form.
CANONICAL FILE LAYOUT is mandatory — never invent helpers.py / utils.py / service.py; the service file is named after the DOMAIN (locker_service.py, parking_service.py, booking_service.py).
NO `...` anywhere in §6 code, no abbreviations like `# ... rest of implementation`; every method body fully implemented (no pass/... bodies even in the service).
Single domain exception base only — never raise a bare RuntimeError/Exception/ValueError from your own code; one specific subclass per failure mode.
Imports at TOP only — never a mid-file `import threading`. Python 3.11+ idioms (list[Foo], X | None); do NOT add `from __future__ import annotations`.

Concurrency test must assert UNIQUE RESOURCES (len({…compartment_id…}) == K), not just the win count — this catches the silent collision bug; use threading.Barrier and pytest.raises(<TypedError>), never bare Exception.
If main.py or pytest errors, fix the CODE — not the test — and re-run before continuing. Both must be green.
strategies.py is included ONLY if §5 has a Strategy pattern; gateways.py ONLY if §5 names an external-system interface (PaymentGateway/NotificationSink/Clock).
No dead code: every public method must be exercised by main.py or a test.
Run model is claude-opus-4-8; the LLD response is the long-form artifact the user READS in the frontend at https://localhost:8002/?s=<uuid>:lld — it must be complete, not summarized.
Code blocks for §6 must be tagged ```python copy (with the `copy` token) so the frontend renders a copy button.

The full §1–§9 markdown must appear BOTH in messages[1] (assistant text, Anthropic format) AND as the first text block (k:text, md:...) of transcript[1].blocks, so follow-ups have context and the reader sees it.

sections.title: Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions
sections.summary: Plain-words restatement (1.1), candidate-LED clarifying proposals as You/Interviewer turns (1.2), and a numbered Functional Requirements + Out-of-scope whiteboard spec (1.3). Every requirement must trace back to the prompt or a Q&A turn; assumptions stated explicitly with 'I'll assume'. Ends with a '💬 What you say to open' line. Preceded by a mandatory '⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan' table and a two-interview-mode choice (Mode A whiteboard/no-run vs Mode B coding env).

sections.title: Actors & Core Flows
sections.summary: Short text-only framing section: a bulleted Actors list (who initiates flows) plus 3-5 numbered happy-path flows written as one-line arrow chains. No system-context or sequence diagram here (that lives in §8).

sections.title: Core Entities (with explicit accept/reject reasoning)
sections.summary: The object-modeling judgment section: walk every candidate noun and explicitly ACCEPT or REJECT it with a one-line reason (3.1), a final entity table with Responsibility / typed Key fields / Invariant-it-guards + an Enums block (3.2), and a flowing spoken-intro paragraph that closes on the rejected nouns (3.3).

sections.title: Class Design (per-class derivation, then the diagram)
sections.summary: Per-class derivation orchestrator-first (4.1) with a 4-block format per class (role / state-derivation table / operations-derivation table / design-choice Q&A), then the consolidated classDiagram (4.2) that is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH matching §3/§6/§7 one-for-one. Splits what to DRAW (5-6 logic-bearing boxes) vs SAY (data bags). Every relationship typed with multiplicity, a Pattern Map table, a box-by-box script, and a PROACTIVE CONCURRENCY FLAG said right after the diagram.

sections.title: Design Patterns & Principles
sections.summary: Lead with Information Expert reasoning (5.1), then 1-3 EARNED patterns each with name/why-it-fits-here/interface+impl/script/layman gloss (5.2), 2-3 SOLID principles tied to named classes (5.3), and the non-negotiable EARN YOUR PATTERNS rule (5.4). Most LLDs need 1-3 patterns; 6 is a yellow flag. Zero patterns is a valid, stronger answer than name-dropping.

sections.title: Core Algorithms & Approach
sections.summary: The bridge between class diagram and code. The Key Insight with why-it-works + what-breaks-without-it (A); per-operation pseudocode + story-driven script + a dry run for the one pipeline method (B); data-structure choices with rejected alternatives in a 4-column table (C); tricky logic as Claim/Proof/Fix triplets (D); the single 'what I'd write WRONG first' anti-example (E); and the §6 narration order table with literal open-lines (F). Every method in §6 must appear here as pseudocode.

sections.title: Implementation — narrated, class by class
sections.summary: Production-quality, fully type-hinted Python 3.11+ in the canonical file layout (models.py, optional strategies.py/gateways.py, <domain>_service.py, main.py, tests/). This is the CLEAN single-threaded core — NO locks here. Opens with §6.0 Live Writing Order playbook (9 phases + 7 golden rules + TL;DR budget + mid-flight situations). Orchestrator presented skeleton-then-flesh, each public method in the mandatory 6-block format; entities as single blocks. Includes one Approach-comparison helper, helper methods, a Verification dry-run subsection, and a Complexity Summary table. No dead code, no '...'/'pass' bodies, single domain base exception.

sections.title: Putting It Together (verified run + pytest suite)
sections.summary: Two deliverables, both run green: main.py narrated demo driver exercising 3-5 happy/tricky cases with assert (7.1), and tests/test_<domain>.py pytest suite covering every public method, every typed-exception path, a boundary case, idempotency, and the STRICT-assertion concurrency test (7.2). Must show actual pytest -q and python3 main.py output; fix the code (not the test) if red.

sections.title: Key Flow (sequence diagram)
sections.summary: A single sequenceDiagram of the most interesting end-to-end flow, plus: one sentence on why this flow was chosen, an arrow-by-arrow walkthrough script narrated like a story, and one sentence on why the sequence proves the design is good (e.g. orchestrator coordinates but delegates all decisions).

sections.title: Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility
sections.summary: DEFAULT is to DESCRIBE verbally; write thread-safe code only if asked. Names the exact race, the lock solution, the trade-off, and the distributed CAS case; includes the concurrency vocabulary table, concurrency follow-up Q&A, the no-op _lock hook + thread-safe subclass (Part A/B/C with full no-'...' method bodies and a Barrier-based N-thread test), an edge-cases list, the extensibility/interviewer-twists subsection (calibration note + summary table + 2-3 full twist walkthroughs), 4-6 hardest follow-ups (concurrency/distributed/scale/undo), and a 30-second final verbal summary.

prompt.concurrencyRequirements:
A generated session MUST treat concurrency as first-class — it is "every LLD problem." Required elements, drawn from <rigor> #6, §4.2, §5.5, §6, §7.2, and §9:

ONE MODEL, APPLIED CONSISTENTLY (rigor #6): (a) state the model — single-threaded event loop, OR one thread/actor per entity, OR a lock-guarded shared object; (b) guard ALL shared mutable reads AND writes, not just some; (c) make check-then-act sequences atomic (select-and-assign together); (d) use a REENTRANT lock if a guarded method may call another guarded method, and say so; (e) NEVER claim a concurrency model you did not actually implement.

PROACTIVE FLAG (the single highest-value signal, repeated in the 60-min plan, §4.2, and §6.0 Phase 3): immediately after presenting the class diagram / state-machine entity, the candidate says (unprompted): "I notice there's a race condition in [main operation] — if two users call it at the same time both see 'available', both proceed, we double-book. I'll keep the core clean and single-threaded and show the fix in §9." Saying this early and confidently is described as the thing that makes interviewers flag "strong hire."

CLEAN CORE / SECOND VERSION SPLIT: §6/§7 must be lock-free and readable (functional correctness only); concurrency hardening is a deliberate SECOND version in §9 via the no-op _lock hook + thread-safe subclass (Template Method) so the diff is ~3-10 lines. notify/IO must stay OUTSIDE the lock so slow SMS never blocks.

EXPERT MOVES to deploy (from <expert_code_patterns>): Move 2 (do-nothing _lock context manager overridden in a subclass — zero lock code in the core file), Move 3 (lock the map/dict of locks before the per-resource lock — the double-lock idiom), Move 5 (threading.Barrier so all N threads hit the critical section at the same instant — without it there's no real race).

CONCURRENCY VOCABULARY (mandatory table in §9 — candidate must define each in plain words AND point to where it bites in THIS problem): race condition, critical section, check-then-act, atomic, mutex/lock, reentrant (RLock), lock granularity (coarse vs fine/per-resource), pessimistic locking, optimistic locking, compare-and-set (CAS), deadlock, starvation.

CONCURRENCY FOLLOW-UP Q&A (4-6, with exact spoken answers): why RLock not Lock; why per-resource not one global lock; deadlock risk (only ever hold ONE lock at a time); pessimistic vs optimistic choice; read-write lock applicability; lock-holder crash mid-critical-section. Plus the distributed case: in-process lock protects only one process, so push atomicity to the DB with an optimistic compare-and-set — UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND status='AVAILABLE' — whoever's update affects 0 rows lost and retries.

STRICT CONCURRENCY TEST (§7.2 + §9 Part C): N-thread / K-capacity race using threading.Barrier with EXACT post-state assertions, not just a count — assert len(wins)==K; assert len(set(wins))==K (unique reservation ids); assert len({w.compartment_id for w in winners})==K (each winner a DIFFERENT compartment — this catches collisions on one box); assert len(losses)==N-K and every loss is the specific typed error; assert count of in-RESERVED/OCCUPIED entities (via the entity's own status field) ==K. The single-count assertion is explicitly called NECESSARY BUT NOT SUFFICIENT.
prompt.extensibilityRequirements:
§9 requires an "Extensibility — interviewer twists" subsection in the hellointerview style (described as MUCH stronger than a flat table), structured in three parts:

(i) LEVEL-CALIBRATION NOTE — one short paragraph stating the depth/quantity of extensibility follow-ups tracks the candidate's target level: junior candidates often get none, mid-level one or two, senior several with depth. Frames the section honestly and tells the candidate how much to prepare.

(ii) SUMMARY TABLE (at-a-glance proof) — a 3-column table: Want to add | How (one class/method) | What in the core changes. Every row MUST read "zero core changes" or "one line" — anything more is a design smell to call out explicitly. Kept tight (4-6 rows); it's the index, twists are the detail. This proves the Strategy + Observer + Repository seams make new rules additive.

(iii) 2-3 TWIST WALKTHROUGHS (the senior signal) — the 2-3 most likely "can your design evolve?" follow-ups for THIS problem, each EVOLVING the design (new feature/state/flow), NOT concurrency or scale. Each twist has FOUR parts in order: the interviewer's exact question; a one-sentence framing of why it's interesting / what currently doesn't handle it; 🎙️ "What you say" (2-3 plain-English sentences naming the exact class/method that changes); "The change" — a SMALL python/pseudocode block showing ONLY the changed lines (diff-shaped minimal change, not the whole file); and "Trade-off" — one line on what it costs and when you'd actually do it vs keep it simple.

Good twist categories to pick from: a fallback/relaxation of an allocation rule (small package uses a larger compartment — walk sizes upward); a new entity state (OUT_OF_SERVICE / maintenance — add enum value + one-line allocation guard); a single-phase → two-phase split (split deposit into reserve + confirm with a RESERVED state + timeout auto-cancel); a new pluggable policy (new Strategy subclass, zero core change). Each twist must demonstrate the design BENDS without breaking — the payoff of the §5 patterns.

Separately, §9 also requires HARDEST FOLLOW-UP QUESTIONS (4-6, DISTINCT from the twists, no overlap) covering concurrency, distribution and scale — MUST include: (1) the hardest concurrency question for this problem, (2) a distributed-systems question ("multiple app servers?"), (3) a scale question ("10M resources?"), and (4) an undo/compensate question ("operation fails halfway through a multi-step process?"). Each with the exact 2-4 sentence spoken answer mapping to a specific class/method.

The whole extensibility bar rests on the EARN YOUR PATTERNS seams actually existing in §6 code: future-only points must be labeled "extension point (not wired in v1)".
prompt.patternsPolicy:
EARN YOUR PATTERNS is stated as a top-level rigor rule (#9: "EARN YOUR PATTERNS & PRINCIPLES — only claim a design pattern or SOLID principle if it is actually realized in the code you show. If you say 'extensible via Observer/Strategy', wire the seam. Do not overstate SOLID.") and is the non-negotiable rule of §5.4. Concrete requirements:

- A pattern counts as "applied" ONLY if its interface/seam is VISIBLE in the §6/§7 code as a real interface with a real implementation, a real Observer registration, or a real State transition. If the seam isn't in the code, it is NOT applied. Every claim must survive Cmd-F — the interviewer WILL ask you to point at the pattern in the code.

- Future-only extension points must be explicitly labeled "extension point (not wired in v1)" — do NOT name-drop them under applied patterns.

- Do NOT claim Singleton unless a single instance is actually enforced (private constructor, getInstance, or DI scope). A class that merely happens to have one instance is a Facade — call it that.

- Do NOT claim Factory for `new Foo(...)`. A Factory is a method/class whose JOB is to translate one type into another (e.g. enum → strategy). No translation = it's just construction, not a Factory.

- Quantity discipline (§5 intro): name only the SMALL number of patterns that actually shape THIS design (most LLDs need 1-3 max). Listing 6 patterns is an explicit yellow flag signaling you don't know which are load-bearing.

- ZERO patterns is a VALID and stronger answer than name-dropping an unused one: "I didn't reach for a GoF pattern here; it's plain Information Expert with three focused classes. If we needed to swap [behavior], I'd add a Strategy seam at [point]."

- Information Expert must be stated FIRST (§5.1) as the underlying reasoning (who owns which state and why) before any GoF pattern — without it the patterns read as buzzwords.

- SOLID must be specific, not "this respects all of SOLID" (empty); pick 2-3 principles tied to NAMED classes.

- Reinforced elsewhere: <expert_code_patterns> says state the ONE smart insight specific to the problem and let the code reflect it; the self_review pass requires verifying "Did you actually implement every pattern/principle you claim?"; §4.2 requires a Pattern Map table binding each class to its pattern with a one-line reason; and the §4/§5/§6/§7 artifacts must match one-for-one (no class/field on the diagram that isn't implemented, and vice-versa).

frontend.toolBlockContract:
A tool block lives inside a saved assistant turn's `blocks[]` array. The frontend's discriminant field is `k` (in `renderSavedAssistant`, app.js ~lines 985-998), with three values: `"text"`, `"tool"`, and `"web"`. A `k:"tool"` block has these exact fields: `id` (string; if absent the frontend synthesizes `"t" + random`), `name` (the tool name string), `input` (object), `output` (string), and `is_error` (boolean, coerced via `!!b.is_error`). On restore the frontend replays it by calling `addToolCall(tab, {id, name: b.name, input: b.input || {}})` then `addToolResult(tab, {id, name: b.name, output: b.output || "", is_error: !!b.is_error})`.

The per-tool `input` contract is defined in `targetForTool(name, inp)` (app.js ~lines 355-360) and `addToolCall` (~362-399):
- write_file: `input.path` (string filename) and `input.content` (string file body). `path` becomes `tool-target`; `content` is rendered as a code block AND captured into `turn.files` as `{path, content}` (dedup by path, last write wins) which powers the full-code viewer. TOOL_META key `write_file` -> cls "write", icon "✎".
- run_command: `input.command` (string). `path` is NOT used; `command` becomes `tool-target` and is shown as `$ <command>` in a `<pre>`. TOOL_META key `run_command` -> cls "run", icon "$".
- read_file / run_python: also key off `input.path`. run_python additionally reads `input.stdin` (rendered under a "stdin" io-label).
- list_files: no target field used.

This exactly matches the server producer in backend/store.py `TranscriptBuilder.consume()` which emits `{"k":"tool","id":...,"name":...,"input":...,"output":None,"is_error":False}` and later fills `output`/`is_error` from the matching tool_result event. The `web` block is `{"k":"web","query": <string>}`. The `text` block is `{"k":"text","md": <markdown string>}`.

Live (streaming) tool events use a parallel shape (not blocks): `tool_call` event = `{type, id, name, input}`; `tool_result` event = `{type, id, name, output, is_error}`.
frontend.viewerBehavior:
There are TWO distinct full-code viewers, both fed from the SAME data source `turn.files` (an array of `{path, content}` captured during streaming from every write_file tool_call, deduped by path with last-write-wins — see addToolCall app.js ~376-382). Dispatch happens in `attachFullCode(turn, mode)` (app.js ~718-729), called on `turn_done` and on restore. (A) The INLINE tabbed "Complete Code Implementation" viewer — `renderInlineFullCode(turn)` (app.js ~735-801). This is the hellointerview-style viewer the question asks about. It builds a `
` appended to `turn.body`, containing: - `.ifc-head` with `

Complete Code Implementation

` and an `.ifc-copyall` "copy all" button. - `.ifc-blurb` static text ("While most companies only require pseudocode... Below is the complete working implementation, file by file."). - `.ifc-viewer` containing `.ifc-tabs` (role="tablist") and a single `.ifc-pane`. Each file becomes a `
frontend.conversationJsonShape:
A loaded conversation (GET /api/conversations/<id>, consumed in restoreConversation app.js ~1005-1041 and produced by backend/store.py `save()`) is a JSON object with top-level keys:
- `session_id` (string, encoded as "<uuid>:<mode>")
- `mode` (string: assessment | interview | lld | hld | behavioral)
- `title` (string, trimmed to 120 chars, defaults "Untitled")
- `created_at`, `updated_at` (epoch seconds)
- `messages[]` — the raw API message log (the model's own conversation history; NOT what the UI renders directly)
- `transcript[]` — the coalesced, render-ready view the UI actually replays.

`transcript[]` is a flat list of role items. Each item has `role` ("user" or "assistant"):
USER item: `{role:"user", text:<string>, images:<int count>}`. The frontend renders it via addUserMessage, prefixing `📎 <n> image(s)\n` when `images` is truthy.
ASSISTANT item: `{role:"assistant", thinking:<string>, blocks:[...], usage:<obj|null>}`. renderSavedAssistant: if `item.thinking` is set it renders a collapsed reasoning block; then iterates `item.blocks || []`; finally `attachFullCode` and `showUsage(item.usage)`.

`blocks[]` (discriminated by `k`):
- `{k:"text", md:<markdown>}` -> rendered through renderMarkdown into a `.prose` div.
- `{k:"tool", id, name, input, output, is_error}` -> replayed as a tool card + result.
- `{k:"web", query}` -> a web-search card.

The LIST endpoint (GET /api/conversations?mode=<mode>) returns `{conversations:[...]}` where each entry is metadata only: `{session_id, mode, title, updated_at, turns}` — `turns` = count of user items in the transcript. The frontend computes a change signature from `session_id:updated_at:turns` to poll-refresh history without repainting. `usage` (when present) has `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, optional `cache_read_input_tokens` (showUsage app.js ~466-474).
frontend.modeHandling:
Mode is one of five values held in the `tabs` object and on each tab as `tab.mode`: `assessment`, `interview`, `lld`, `hld`, `behavioral` (app.js ~73-79; PLACEHOLDERS ~52-58). The active mode is also encoded into the session id as the suffix after a colon: `newSessionId(mode)` = `rawId() + ":" + mode`, and on load the mode is recovered with `urlSession.split(":").pop()` (~1204). The conversation JSON also carries `mode` at top level, used by restoreConversation to switch tabs.

The single place mode changes RENDERING is `attachFullCode(turn, mode)` (app.js ~718-729). The check is literally `if (mode === "lld")`:
- lld mode: hide the `.full-code-btn` (`btn.hidden = true`) and instead call `renderInlineFullCode(turn)` to inject the inline tabbed "Complete Code Implementation" section directly into the answer body. The code comment states "In LLD mode we render a hellointerview-style 'Complete Code Implementation' section INLINE at the end of the answer — collapsible per file, no modal. In every other mode the existing 📂 full code button reveals the modal."
- all other modes (assessment/interview/hld/behavioral): leave the inline section off and set `btn.hidden = !hasFiles`, so the `📂 full code` button (opening the modal) appears only if files were written.

This dispatch runs identically on both the live `turn_done` path (handleEvent ~508) and the restore/live-sync path (renderSavedAssistant ~1001), so saved lld conversations re-render the inline viewer too. Beyond full-code dispatch, mode also selects: the composer placeholder text (PLACEHOLDERS), and the per-mode follow-up `QUICK_ACTIONS` button set (app.js ~298-333; lld's set is Design Patterns / Sequence Diagram / Error Handling / Unit Tests). The CSS for the inline viewer (`.inline-fullcode`, `.ifc-*`) is documented in styles.css ~871-965 as "(LLD mode only)". Note also the separate LP Coach tab (`lp-tab`) is a special iframe embed that bypasses the session/streaming system entirely and is not part of the five modes.

serving.convDir: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations (CONV_DIR). Defined in backend/config.py line 43: CONV_DIR = Path(os.environ.get("CONV_DIR", PROJECT_ROOT / "data" / "conversations")).resolve(). PROJECT_ROOT is the folder containing the backend/ package (config.py line 18). Overridable via the CONV_DIR env var. One JSON file per session lives here.
serving.slugFormat: Defined by config.session_slug(session_id) in backend/config.py lines 46-48: re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9_-]", "_", session_id or "").strip("._")[:80] or "session". I.e. replace every char that is NOT [A-Za-z0-9_-] with an underscore, strip leading/trailing '.' and '_', cap at 80 chars, and fall back to the literal "session" if the result is empty. The on-disk conversation filename is f"{session_slug(session_id)}.json" (store.py _path, lines 20-21). In practice session_id is "<uuid>_<mode>" (e.g. 03fdfb5b-dd34-4ecb-b3b3-5cfdf59b8edf_interview) which is already slug-safe, so the file is e.g. 03fdfb5b-dd34-4ecb-b3b3-5cfdf59b8edf_interview.json. Saves are atomic: written to <slug>.json.tmp then .replace()d onto <slug>.json (store.py lines 48-52).</slug></mode></slug>
serving.workspaceDir: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace (WORKSPACE_DIR). Defined in backend/config.py line 38: WORKSPACE_DIR = Path(os.environ.get("WORKSPACE_DIR", PROJECT_ROOT / "workspace")).resolve(). Overridable via the WORKSPACE_DIR env var. Per-conversation subdirs are created by config.workspace_for(session_id, mode) (config.py lines 51-61): WORKSPACE_DIR / session_slug(session_id) when a session_id is given, else WORKSPACE_DIR / (mode or "default"). The per-session subdir uses the SAME slug as the conversation filename, so workspace/<slug>/ pairs with data/conversations/<slug>.json (e.g. workspace/060c4a0e-...-hld/ and data/conversations/060c4a0e-...-hld.json). This is where the agent writes/runs files, confined to WORKSPACE_DIR.
serving.howServed:
A FastAPI app in backend/server.py serves both the JSON API and the static SPA. Three conversation endpoints (all gated by _check_token via the optional X-Algora-Token header; open if ALGORA_TOKEN unset):
- GET /api/conversations (server.py lines 321-328) -> calls store.list_all() and returns {"conversations": [...]}. Optional ?mode=<mode> query filters by mode. list_all() (store.py lines 55-75) globs CONV_DIR/*.json, reads each, and returns lightweight metadata per file: session_id, mode, title, updated_at, and turns (count of user-role transcript items), sorted by updated_at descending (newest first).
- GET /api/conversations/{session_id} (server.py lines 331-343) -> store.load(session_id) and returns {session_id, mode, title, transcript}. The 'transcript' is the coalesced render-ready view (user/assistant blocks) built by store.TranscriptBuilder; returns 404 if the file is missing.
- DELETE /api/conversations/{session_id} (server.py lines 346-353) -> drops the in-memory Agent from _sessions and calls store.delete() (unlinks the file).
The static frontend (config.FRONTEND_DIR = /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/frontend) is mounted LAST at "/" via StaticFiles(html=True) (server.py line 482) so /api/* routes take precedence. Chat itself streams over SSE at POST /api/chat; each turn is persisted to disk by _persist_turn / the _run_agent task in _stream_events so conversations survive restarts and client disconnects.
serving.sessionIdToFile: store._path(session_id) -> config.CONV_DIR / f"{config.session_slug(session_id)}.json" (store.py lines 20-21). So a session_id maps to its file by slugifying it (see slugFormat) and appending .json under CONV_DIR. store.load() reads CONV_DIR/<slug>.json and json.loads it (returns None if missing/corrupt); store.save() writes it atomically (<slug>.json.tmp then rename). The reverse direction (file -> session) is also self-describing: each JSON file stores its own "session_id" field, which list_all() and get_conversation() echo back. Confirmed on disk: filenames are "<uuid>_<mode>.json" (e.g. 03fdfb5b-dd34-4ecb-b3b3-5cfdf59b8edf_interview.json), matching the slug of session_id "<uuid>_<mode>".</mode></uuid>
